papers.title: Beyond IID: Optimizing Instruction Learning from the Perspective of Instruction Interaction and Dependency
papers.abstract: With the availability of various instruction datasets, a pivotal challenge is how to effectively select and integrate these instructions to fine-tune large language models (LLMs). Previous research mainly focuses on selecting individual high-quality instructions. However, these works overlooked the joint interactions and dependencies between different categories of instructions, leading to suboptimal selection strategies. Moreover, the nature of these interaction patterns remains largely unexplored, let alone optimize the instruction set with regard to them. To fill these gaps, in this paper, we: (1) systemically investigate interaction and dependency patterns between different categories of instructions, (2) manage to optimize the instruction set concerning the interaction patterns using a linear programming-based method, and optimize the learning schema of SFT using an instruction dependency taxonomy guided curriculum learning. Experimental results across different LLMs demonstrate improved performance over strong baselines on widely adopted benchmarks.
papers.affiliations: Beijing Academy of Artificial Intelligence

significance.reasoning: While SFT data selection isn't our primary focus, the formulation of data mixing as a Linear Programming problem based on an 'interaction matrix' is a perfect example of 'OR for AI'. We could adapt this LP approach to optimize population diversity in AlgoEvo or prompt buffers in our multi-agent systems.

papers.brief: The authors propose optimizing SFT data mixtures using Linear Programming (EE-CPO) by modeling the 'interaction' (synergy/antagonism) between instruction categories, rather than treating them as IID. They empirically derive a dependency taxonomy showing Math and Code are fundamental 'root' capabilities required before learning complex tasks, validating this via curriculum learning experiments that beat DEITA. The results are solid (+1.73 AlpacaEval over DEITA), though the cost of deriving the interaction matrix (training N models) is high. **Takeaway:** The 'Effect Equivalence Coefficient' matrix combined with an LP solver is a rigorous OR formulation for resource/data allocation that we should steal to optimize heuristic populations in our evolutionary search frameworks.

methodology.core_method: Causal Intervention based Instruction Correlation Analysis and Ability Taxonomy Induction; Effect Equivalence-based Linear Programming for Category Proportion Optimization (EE-CPO); Dependency Taxonomy Guided Curriculum Supervised Fine-Tuning (DT-CSFT)
methodology.novelty_claim: Systematically investigates instruction interaction and dependency patterns and optimizes instruction sets via linear programming and curriculum learning.
components: Causal Intervention based Instruction Correlation Analysis
Causal Intervention based Ability Taxonomy Induction

papers.title: A Survey of Optimization Modeling Meets LLMs: Progress and Future Directions
papers.abstract: By virtue of its great utility in solving real-world problems, optimization modeling has been widely employed for optimal decision-making across various sectors, but it requires substantial expertise from operations research professionals. With the advent of large language models (LLMs), new opportunities have emerged to automate the procedure of mathematical modeling. This survey presents a comprehensive and timely review of recent advancements that cover the entire technical stack, including data synthesis and fine-tuning for the base model, inference frameworks, benchmark datasets, and performance evaluation. In addition, we conducted an in-depth analysis on the quality of benchmark datasets, which was found to have a surprisingly high error rate. We cleaned the datasets and constructed a new leaderboard with fair performance evaluation in terms of base LLM model and datasets. We also build an online portal that integrates resources of cleaned datasets, code and paper repository to benefit the community. Finally, we identify limitations in current methodologies and outline future research opportunities.
papers.affiliations: Zhejiang University, Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab, Singapore University of Social Sciences, Hangzhou High-Tech Zone (Binjiang) Institute of Blockchain and Data Security

significance.reasoning: This paper invalidates the baselines of several major datasets (IndustryOR, NL4Opt) by identifying massive error rates (up to 54%). As we are developing 'OR-Bench', ignoring this cleaning effort would render our own benchmarking comparisons scientifically flawed.

papers.brief: This survey and empirical audit reveals that standard optimization modeling benchmarks (NL4Opt, IndustryOR) suffer from critical error rates ranging from 16% to 54%, rendering prior leaderboards unreliable. The authors manually cleaned these datasets and re-evaluated methods, finding that Chain-of-Thought (CoT) often degrades performance compared to standard prompting, while fine-tuned models (ORLM) and multi-agent systems (Chain-of-Experts) perform best. The immediate takeaway is that we must adopt their cleaned datasets for our OR-Bench project; using the original open-source versions is no longer defensible. Additionally, the failure of CoT on these tasks suggests we should prioritize multi-agent or fine-tuned approaches for symbolic formulation tasks.

methodology.core_method: Systematic Literature Review and Empirical Re-evaluation
methodology.novelty_claim: First systematic review of LLM-based optimization modeling, including data cleaning of existing benchmarks, construction of a new leaderboard, and an online resource portal.

papers.title: OptiMUS-0.3: Using Large Language Models to Model and Solve Optimization Problems at Scale
papers.abstract: Optimization problems are pervasive in sectors from manufacturing and distribution to healthcare. However, most such problems are still solved heuristically by hand rather than optimally by state-of-the-art solvers because the expertise required to formulate and solve these problems limits the widespread adoption of optimization tools and techniques. We introduce a Large Language Model (LLM)-based system designed to formulate and solve (mixed integer) linear programming problems from their natural language descriptions. Our system is capable of developing mathematical models, writing and debugging solver code, evaluating the generated solutions, and improving efficiency and correctness of its model and code based on these evaluations. OptiMUS-0.3 utilizes a modular structure to process problems, allowing it to handle problems with long descriptions and complex data without long prompts. Experiments demonstrate that OptiMUS-0.3 outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on easy datasets by more than 22% and on hard datasets (including a new dataset, NLP4LP, released with this paper that features long and complex problems) by more than 24%.

significance.reasoning: This paper sets the current SOTA for LLM-to-MILP formulation, directly impacting our 'OR-Bench' and 'Generative AI for OR' tracks. The modular architecture (specifically the connection graph and structure detection) offers a concrete blueprint for handling the large-scale instances and complex descriptions that currently break our baselines.

papers.brief: OptiMUS-0.3 is a modular multi-agent system that translates natural language into Gurobi code, utilizing a 'connection graph' to manage variable-constraint relationships in long contexts and specialized agents to detect solver-specific structures (SOS, indicators) or implement sifting. The results are rigorous, introducing a new hard benchmark (NLP4LP) where they outperform GPT-4o by ~40% and beat Chain-of-Experts. The most stealable insight is the 'Structure Detection Agent': instead of relying on the LLM to write generic constraints, we should explicitly prompt for and map high-level structures to efficient solver APIs (like SOS constraints) to improve performance in our EvoCut and AlgoEvo pipelines. This is a necessary read for the OR-Bench team.

methodology.core_method: Modular LLM-based agent (OptiMUS-0.3) employing a connection graph and self-reflective error correction for sequential optimization model formulation and code generation using Gurobi API
methodology.novelty_claim: OptiMUS-0.3 employs a modular structure with a connection graph to process constraints and objectives independently, enabling it to handle long descriptions and large data without excessively long prompts, and incorporates self-reflective error correction and advanced optimization modeling techniques.

papers.title: Improving GPU Multi-Tenancy Through Dynamic Multi-Instance GPU Reconfiguration
papers.abstract: Continuous learning (CL) has emerged as one of the most popular deep learning paradigms deployed in modern cloud GPUs. Specifically, CL has the capability to continuously update the model parameters (through model retraining) and use the updated model (if available) to serve overtime arriving inference requests. It is generally beneficial to co-locate the retraining and inference together to enable timely model updates and avoid model transfer overheads. This brings the need for GPU sharing among retraining and inferences. Meanwhile, multiple CL workloads can share the modern GPUs in the cloud, leading to multi-tenancy execution. In this paper, we observe that prior GPU-sharing techniques are not optimized for multi-tenancy CL workloads. Specifically, they do not coherently consider the accuracy of the retraining model and the inference service level objective (SLO) attainment. Moreover, they cannot accommodate the overtime dynamics (e.g., inference arrival intensity) in CL execution. In this paper, we propose MIGRator, a novel GPU reconfiguration runtime that dynamically performs GPU reconfiguration for multi-tenancy CL workloads. MIGRator is based on the recent NVIDIA multi-instance GPU (MIG) to mitigate resource contention and formulates the reconfiguration optimization into Integer Linear Programming (ILP) to dynamically identify, reconfigure, and allocate the GPU instances. MIGRator leverages the "Goodput" metric in the ILP objective function to consider both inference SLO attainment and model accuracy in the reconfiguration exploration. We evaluate MIGRator using representative multi-tenancy CL workloads. The results show our approach outperforms the state-of-the-art GPU sharing techniques (i.e., Ekya, Astraea, and PARIS) by 17\%, 21\%, and 20\%, respectively.
papers.affiliations: UC San Diego, University of Pittsburgh, University of Arizona, University of Georgia

significance.reasoning: This paper is a direct reference for our 'GPUSched' project. It demonstrates that fine-grained MIG reconfiguration (usually considered too expensive due to ~6s latency) is viable if modeled correctly within an ILP that accounts for reconfiguration penalties.

papers.brief: MIGRator formulates dynamic NVIDIA MIG partitioning as an Integer Linear Program (ILP) to optimize a compound 'Goodput' metric (SLO + accuracy) for continuous learning workloads. The results on A100s show ~20% gains over baselines like Ekya and PARIS, largely by mitigating the massive ~6s MIG reconfiguration overhead via a 'pre-initialization' lookahead strategy. For our GPUSched project, the key takeaway is the explicit modeling of reconfiguration penalties in the ILP and the technique of pre-assembling instances during idle time to hide latency. While the reliance on 200-second traffic prediction is a potential fragility, the rigorous handling of hardware constraints makes this a strong reference for our OR-based resource allocation work.

methodology.core_method: Integer Linear Programming (ILP) for dynamic Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) reconfiguration with Goodput objective
methodology.novelty_claim: MIGRator is a novel GPU reconfiguration runtime that dynamically performs GPU reconfiguration for multi-tenancy CL workloads, leveraging the 'Goodput' metric in the ILP objective function to consider both inference SLO attainment and model accuracy.

papers.title: Understanding the Importance of Evolutionary Search in Automated Heuristic Design with Large Language Models
papers.abstract: Automated heuristic design (AHD) has gained considerable attention for its potential to automate the development of effective heuristics. The recent advent of large language models (LLMs) has paved a new avenue for AHD, with initial efforts focusing on framing AHD as an evolutionary program search (EPS) problem. However, inconsistent benchmark settings, inadequate baselines, and a lack of detailed component analysis have left the necessity of integrating LLMs with search strategies and the true progress achieved by existing LLM-based EPS methods to be inadequately justified. This work seeks to fulfill these research queries by conducting a large-scale benchmark comprising four LLM-based EPS methods and four AHD problems across nine LLMs and five independent runs. Our extensive experiments yield meaningful insights, providing empirical grounding for the importance of evolutionary search in LLM-based AHD approaches, while also contributing to the advancement of future EPS algorithmic development. To foster accessibility and reproducibility, we have fully open-sourced our benchmark and corresponding results.
papers.affiliations: City University of Hong Kong, Southern University of Science and Technology

significance.reasoning: This paper exposes a critical weakness in the field: complex LLM-evolution methods (FunSearch, ReEvo) often fail to significantly outperform a trivial (1+1)-ES hill climber. We must validate our AlgoEvo/EvoCut architectures against this simple baseline immediately to justify our complexity.

papers.brief: Zhang et al. perform a rigorous benchmarking of major LLM-based evolutionary program search (EPS) methods (FunSearch, EoH, ReEvo) against a simple (1+1)-EPS baseline across four problems and nine LLMs. The results are empirically solid and sobering: the simple (1+1)-EPS baseline—iterative improvement via one-shot prompting—frequently matches or outperforms the complex population-based methods, particularly on bin packing, though EoH remains superior on TSP. **Crucial Takeaway:** We are likely over-engineering our search mechanisms; we must implement a (1+1)-EPS baseline in all future experiments (AlgoEvo, EvoCut) because if our multi-agent systems cannot beat this simple hill-climber, our papers will be rejected for unnecessary complexity. Additionally, they find that larger models (GPT-4) do not strictly guarantee better heuristic search performance compared to smaller, code-specialized models like CodeLlama-7B.

methodology.llm_model_used: UniXcoder, StarCoder, CodeLlama-7B, CodeLlama-34B, DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B, DeepSeek-Coder-33B, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Claude 3 Opus
methodology.novelty_claim: This work provides empirical grounding for the importance of evolutionary search in LLM-based AHD approaches through a large-scale benchmark and detailed analysis.

papers.title: OptiBench Meets ReSocratic: Measure and Improve LLMs for Optimization Modeling
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have exhibited their problem-solving abilities in mathematical reasoning. Solving realistic optimization (OPT) problems in application scenarios requires advanced and applied mathematics ability. However, current OPT benchmarks that merely solve linear programming are far from complex realistic situations. In this work, we propose OptiBench, a benchmark for End-to-end optimization problem-solving with human-readable inputs and outputs. OptiBench contains rich optimization problems, including linear and nonlinear programming with or without tabular data, which can comprehensively evaluate LLMs' solving ability. In our benchmark, LLMs are required to call a code solver to provide precise numerical answers. Furthermore, to alleviate the data scarcity for optimization problems, and to bridge the gap between open-source LLMs on a small scale (e.g., Llama-3-8b) and closed-source LLMs (e.g., GPT-4), we further propose a data synthesis method namely ReSocratic. Unlike general data synthesis methods that proceed from questions to answers, \ReSocratic first incrementally synthesizes formatted optimization demonstration with mathematical formulations step by step and then back-translates the generated demonstrations into questions. Based on this, we synthesize the ReSocratic-29k dataset. We further conduct supervised fine-tuning with ReSocratic-29k on multiple open-source models. Experimental results show that ReSocratic-29k significantly improves the performance of open-source models.
papers.affiliations: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, ETH Zurich, Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab, City University of Hong Kong, Sun Yat-sen University, MBZUAI, University of California Merced, Chongqing University

significance.reasoning: This paper directly competes with and informs your 'OR-Bench' project. It establishes a new baseline (OptiBench) for LLM optimization modeling that includes nonlinear and tabular data, which previous benchmarks (NL4OPT) lacked. The data synthesis method is directly applicable to your work.

papers.brief: The authors propose OptiBench, a benchmark of 605 optimization problems (linear/nonlinear, tabular/text), and ReSocratic, a data synthesis method that generates formal models first and back-translates them into natural language questions. Results are strong: fine-tuning Llama-3-8B on their 29k synthetic samples improves accuracy from 13.6% to 51.1%, validating the data quality. **Key Takeaway:** The 'Reverse Socratic' synthesis pipeline (Formal Model → Code → NL Question) is the superior strategy for generating synthetic OR datasets because it guarantees solvability and ground truth by construction, unlike forward generation. We should steal this pipeline for generating robust test instances for OR-Bench and potentially for training our OR agents.

methodology.novelty_claim: We propose ReSocratic, a novel method for generating diverse and reliable data for optimization problems, which synthesizes complex reasoning data from scratch in a reverse manner.

papers.title: FunBO: Discovering Acquisition Functions for Bayesian Optimization with FunSearch
papers.abstract: The sample efficiency of Bayesian optimization algorithms depends on carefully crafted acquisition functions (AFs) guiding the sequential collection of function evaluations. The best-performing AF can vary significantly across optimization problems, often requiring ad-hoc and problem-specific choices. This work tackles the challenge of designing novel AFs that perform well across a variety of experimental settings. Based on FunSearch, a recent work using Large Language Models (LLMs) for discovery in mathematical sciences, we propose FunBO, an LLM-based method that can be used to learn new AFs written in computer code by leveraging access to a limited number of evaluations for a set of objective functions. We provide the analytic expression of all discovered AFs and evaluate them on various global optimization benchmarks and hyperparameter optimization tasks. We show how FunBO identifies AFs that generalize well in and out of the training distribution of functions, thus outperforming established general-purpose AFs and achieving competitive performance against AFs that are customized to specific function types and are learned via transfer-learning algorithms.
papers.affiliations: Google DeepMind

significance.reasoning: This is a direct, high-profile application of LLM evolutionary search (FunSearch) to discover optimization algorithms (Acquisition Functions). It validates our core thesis in 'AlgoEvo' but applies it to continuous optimization. We need to benchmark our methods against this approach.

papers.brief: FunBO applies FunSearch to evolve Python code for Bayesian Optimization acquisition functions, evaluating fitness by running full BO loops on synthetic functions. The results are empirically strong, showing that evolved AFs generalize well to out-of-distribution functions and outperform standard baselines like EI and UCB. The most stealable insight is their 'few-shot' adaptation strategy, where a general-purpose heuristic is rapidly fine-tuned on a small set of target instances—a technique we should immediately test for our VRP heuristics. While the method is computationally expensive (brute-forcing the inner loop), the interpretable code outputs provide concrete ideas for dynamic exploration-exploitation trade-offs.

methodology.novelty_claim: This is the first work exploring AFs represented in computer code, thus demonstrating a novel approach to harness the power of LLMs for sampling policy design.

papers.title: Evolution of Heuristics: Towards Efficient Automatic Algorithm Design Using Large Language Model
papers.abstract: Heuristics are widely used for dealing with complex search and optimization problems. However, manual design of heuristics can be often very labour extensive and requires rich working experience and knowledge. This paper proposes Evolution of Heuristic (EoH), a novel evolutionary paradigm that leverages both Large Language Models (LLMs) and Evolutionary Computation (EC) methods for Automatic Heuristic Design (AHD). EoH represents the ideas of heuristics in natural language, termed thoughts. They are then translated into executable codes by LLMs. The evolution of both thoughts and codes in an evolutionary search framework makes it very effective and efficient for generating high-performance heuristics. Experiments on three widely studied combinatorial optimization benchmark problems demonstrate that EoH outperforms commonly used handcrafted heuristics and other recent AHD methods including FunSearch. Particularly, the heuristic produced by EoH with a low computational budget (in terms of the number of queries to LLMs) significantly outperforms widely-used human hand-crafted baseline algorithms for the online bin packing problem.
papers.affiliations: Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab, City University of Hong Kong, Southern University of Science and Technology

significance.reasoning: This paper directly targets our core focus (LLM evolutionary search for OR) and claims to beat FunSearch with orders of magnitude better sample efficiency (2k queries vs millions). The 'Thought + Code' co-evolution mechanism is a concrete architectural change we can immediately test in AlgoEvo to reduce our inference costs.

papers.brief: EoH introduces a dual-track evolutionary framework that evolves both natural language 'thoughts' (heuristic logic) and their corresponding Python code, rather than code alone. On Online Bin Packing, it claims to outperform DeepMind's FunSearch while using only ~2,000 LLM queries (vs FunSearch's millions), and achieves SOTA gaps on TSP and FSSP via Guided Local Search. The critical takeaway is the 'E2' prompt strategy: explicitly asking the LLM to extract common ideas from parent heuristics into a natural language 'thought' before generating code, which acts as a genetic Chain-of-Thought to stabilize mutation. We should immediately implement this 'Thought-then-Code' mutation operator in our AlgoEvo pipeline to address our sample efficiency bottlenecks.

methodology.novelty_claim: We propose EoH, a novel paradigm that uses LLMs to evolution both thoughts and codes for the automatic design of heuristics with minimum hand-craft design and no domain model training.

papers.title: tnGPS: Discovering Unknown Tensor Network Structure Search Algorithms via Large Language Models (LLMs
papers.abstract: Tensor networks are efficient for extremely high-dimensional representation, but their model selection, known as tensor network structure search (TN-SS), is a challenging problem. Although several works have targeted TN-SS, most existing algorithms are manually crafted heuristics with poor performance, suffering from the curse of dimensionality and local convergence. In this work, we jump out of the box, studying how to harness large language models (LLMs) to automatically discover new TN-SS algorithms, replacing the involvement of human experts. By observing how human experts innovate in research, we model their common workflow and propose an automatic algorithm discovery framework called tnGPS. The proposed framework is an elaborate prompting pipeline that instruct LLMs to generate new TN-SS algorithms through iterative refinement and enhancement. The experimental results demonstrate that the algorithms discovered by tnGPS exhibit superior performance in benchmarks compared to the current state-of-the-art methods.
papers.affiliations: RIKEN Center for Advanced Intelligence Project, Tencent Inc., Guangdong University of Technology

significance.reasoning: This paper implements a structured LLM-evolution loop (similar to our AlgoEvo) but explicitly tackles population diversity via LLM-based semantic clustering ('Knowledge Categorization'). This is a concrete, transferable technique to improve the diversity and quality of our own evolutionary search methods.

papers.brief: The authors propose tnGPS, a FunSearch-style framework that evolves Python code for Tensor Network Structure Search by mimicking human innovation stages (categorization, recombination, diversity injection). While the application (Tensor Networks) is niche, the results outperform standard heuristics like TNGA and TNLS. The critical takeaway for us is the 'Knowledge Categorization' phase: they use the LLM to semantically cluster the population of generated algorithms to manage diversity and guide the 'Diversity Injection' step. We should immediately implement this LLM-based population clustering in AlgoEvo to prevent convergence on similar code patterns.

methodology.core_method: LLM-driven automation framework for algorithm discovery via iterative refinement and enhancement using a prompting pipeline
methodology.novelty_claim: We propose tnGPS, a large language model (LLM)-driven automation framework designed to automatically generate novel and effective TN-SS algorithms tailored to specific downstream tasks.

papers.title: LLM Guided Evolution -- The Automation of Models Advancing Models
papers.abstract: In the realm of machine learning, traditional model development and automated approaches like AutoML typically rely on layers of abstraction, such as tree-based or Cartesian genetic programming. Our study introduces "Guided Evolution" (GE), a novel framework that diverges from these methods by utilizing Large Language Models (LLMs) to directly modify code. GE leverages LLMs for a more intelligent, supervised evolutionary process, guiding mutations and crossovers. Our unique "Evolution of Thought" (EoT) technique further enhances GE by enabling LLMs to reflect on and learn from the outcomes of previous mutations. This results in a self-sustaining feedback loop that augments decision-making in model evolution. GE maintains genetic diversity, crucial for evolutionary algorithms, by leveraging LLMs' capability to generate diverse responses from expertly crafted prompts and modulate model temperature. This not only accelerates the evolution process but also injects expert like creativity and insight into the process. Our application of GE in evolving the ExquisiteNetV2 model demonstrates its efficacy: the LLM-driven GE autonomously produced variants with improved accuracy, increasing from 92.52% to 93.34%, without compromising model compactness. This underscores the potential of LLMs to accelerate the traditional model design pipeline, enabling models to autonomously evolve and enhance their own designs.
papers.affiliations: Georgia Tech Research Institute

significance.reasoning: The paper is empirically weak (single trials on CIFAR-10), but the 'Evolution of Thought' (EoT) prompting strategy—using the diff between an elite mutant and its seed to ground future mutations—is a concrete, transferable mechanism for implementing feedback loops in our AlgoEvo framework without training a value model.

papers.brief: Morris et al. propose 'Guided Evolution,' an LLM-based NAS framework that introduces 'Evolution of Thought' (EoT) and 'Character Role Play' to guide code mutations. While the results are statistically negligible (single trials, ~0.8% gain on CIFAR-10), the EoT mechanism offers a specific, actionable prompt engineering technique: explicitly prompting the LLM to compare a successful elite individual against its original seed to extract 'reasoning' before applying mutations to new individuals. This serves as a lightweight, prompt-based memory/feedback mechanism that could immediately improve sample efficiency in our evolutionary search agents. The 'Character Role Play' (e.g., asking the LLM to act as 'Dr. MaGoo' for unorthodox ideas) is a gimmicky but potentially useful heuristic for maintaining population diversity.

methodology.core_method: LLM-guided Genetic Algorithm for Neural Architecture Search (NAS) with multi-objective optimization (accuracy and parameter count), incorporating Evolution of Thought (EoT) and Character Role Play (CRP) for mutation and mating.
methodology.novelty_claim: Our study introduces "Guided Evolution" (GE), a novel framework that utilizes Large Language Models (LLMs) to directly modify code, and "Evolution of Thought" (EoT) which enables LLMs to reflect on and learn from previous mutation outcomes.

papers.title: Evolution Transformer: In-Context Evolutionary Optimization
papers.abstract: Evolutionary optimization algorithms are often derived from loose biological analogies and struggle to leverage information obtained during the sequential course of optimization. An alternative promising approach is to leverage data and directly discover powerful optimization principles via meta-optimization. In this work, we follow such a paradigm and introduce Evolution Transformer, a causal Transformer architecture, which can flexibly characterize a family of Evolution Strategies. Given a trajectory of evaluations and search distribution statistics, Evolution Transformer outputs a performance-improving update to the search distribution. The architecture imposes a set of suitable inductive biases, i.e. the invariance of the distribution update to the order of population members within a generation and equivariance to the order of the search dimensions. We train the model weights using Evolutionary Algorithm Distillation, a technique for supervised optimization of sequence models using teacher algorithm trajectories. The resulting model exhibits strong in-context optimization performance and shows strong generalization capabilities to otherwise challenging neuroevolution tasks. We analyze the resulting properties of the Evolution Transformer and propose a technique to fully self-referentially train the Evolution Transformer, starting from a random initialization and bootstrapping its own learning progress. We provide an open source implementation under https://github.com/RobertTLange/evosax.
papers.affiliations: Google DeepMind, TU Berlin

significance.reasoning: This paper provides a concrete neural architecture and training loop (SR-EAD) for 'learning to optimize' without a teacher. It directly challenges our reliance on static LLM prompts for search logic and offers a blueprint for 'Evolving the Evolver' via self-generated data.

papers.brief: Lange et al. introduce the Evolution Transformer, a causal architecture that learns to perform evolutionary strategy updates by attending to optimization history, effectively 'distilling' algorithms like CMA-ES into a neural network. Crucially, they propose 'Self-Referential Algorithm Distillation' (SR-EAD), where the model improves itself by perturbing its own weights, generating trajectories, and filtering for the best ones to retrain on—eliminating the need for a teacher. The results are strong, showing generalization to unseen Brax control tasks and successful (though sometimes unstable) self-bootstrapping. The key takeaway for us is the SR-EAD loop as a mechanism for open-ended optimizer improvement, and their use of Perceiver cross-attention to handle variable population sizes—a technique we should immediately steal for our multi-agent memory architectures.

methodology.novelty_claim: We introduce Evolution Transformer, an architecture inducing a population-order invariant and dimension-order equivariant search update.

papers.title: LLaMoCo: Instruction Tuning of Large Language Models for Optimization Code Generation
papers.abstract: Recent research explores optimization using large language models (LLMs) by either iteratively seeking next-step solutions from LLMs or directly prompting LLMs for an optimizer. However, these approaches exhibit inherent limitations, including low operational efficiency, high sensitivity to prompt design, and a lack of domain-specific knowledge. We introduce LLaMoCo, the first instruction-tuning framework designed to adapt LLMs for solving optimization problems in a code-to-code manner. Specifically, we establish a comprehensive instruction set containing well-described problem prompts and effective optimization codes. We then develop a novel two-phase learning strategy that incorporates a contrastive learning-based warm-up procedure before the instruction-tuning phase to enhance the convergence behavior during model fine-tuning. The experiment results demonstrate that a CodeGen (350M) model fine-tuned by our LLaMoCo achieves superior optimization performance compared to GPT-4 Turbo and the other competitors across both synthetic and realistic problem sets. The fine-tuned model and the usage instructions are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/LLaMoCo-722A.
papers.affiliations: Singapore Management University, Nanyang Technological University, South China University of Technology

significance.reasoning: This paper demonstrates that a fine-tuned 350M parameter model can drastically outperform GPT-4 Turbo in generating optimization code by learning to select and configure the right solver (e.g., CMA-ES vs. SLSQP) based on problem structure. This directly addresses the scalability bottleneck in our AlgoEvo project, suggesting we should fine-tune local models on our search logs rather than relying on expensive, generic frontier models.

papers.brief: LLaMoCo fine-tunes small LLMs (down to 350M) to generate executable Python optimization code by training on a synthetic dataset where the 'ground truth' is the empirically best-performing solver identified via exhaustive benchmarking. The results are compelling: the fine-tuned 350M model achieves ~85% normalized performance on benchmarks where GPT-4 Turbo only reaches ~14-30%, largely because the small model learns to select specialized evolutionary strategies (like BIPOP-CMA-ES) while GPT-4 defaults to generic gradient-based solvers. **Key Takeaway:** We can replace the expensive GPT-4 calls in our evolutionary search loop with a specialized, fine-tuned local model (CodeLlama-7B) trained on our historical search successes, significantly improving both sample efficiency and scalability. The paper's 'contrastive warm-up' strategy for aligning diverse problem descriptions is also a transferable technique for our problem encoding work.

methodology.core_method: Instruction tuning of LLMs with a two-phase learning strategy incorporating contrastive learning-based warm-up and sequence-to-sequence loss
methodology.novelty_claim: LLaMoCo is the first instruction-tuning framework designed to adapt LLMs for solving optimization problems in a code-to-code manner.

papers.title: OptiMUS: Scalable Optimization Modeling with (MI)LP Solvers and Large Language Models
papers.abstract: Optimization problems are pervasive in sectors from manufacturing and distribution to healthcare. However, most such problems are still solved heuristically by hand rather than optimally by state-of-the-art solvers because the expertise required to formulate and solve these problems limits the widespread adoption of optimization tools and techniques. This paper introduces OptiMUS, a Large Language Model (LLM)-based agent designed to formulate and solve (mixed integer) linear programming problems from their natural language descriptions. OptiMUS can develop mathematical models, write and debug solver code, evaluate the generated solutions, and improve its model and code based on these evaluations. OptiMUS utilizes a modular structure to process problems, allowing it to handle problems with long descriptions and complex data without long prompts. Experiments demonstrate that OptiMUS outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on easy datasets by more than $20\%$ and on hard datasets (including a new dataset, NLP4LP, released with this paper that features long and complex problems) by more than $30\%$.
papers.affiliations: Stanford University

significance.reasoning: Directly competes with/complements our OR-Bench project by releasing the NLP4LP benchmark. The 'Connection Graph' architecture is a concrete, transferable solution to the context-window bottleneck in code generation agents, highly relevant for our AlgoEvo and MAS memory architectures.

papers.brief: OptiMUS is a multi-agent framework for translating natural language into Gurobi code, achieving SOTA performance by using a 'Connection Graph' to map variables and parameters to specific constraints. This graph allows the agents to dynamically filter context and construct minimal prompts, enabling success on problems with long descriptions where baselines like Chain-of-Experts fail. They release NLP4LP, a hard benchmark of 67 complex instances, which we must immediately compare against our OR-Bench efforts. The **Connection Graph** is the key stealable insight: a structured dependency tracking mechanism that solves context pollution in iterative code generation, directly applicable to our AlgoEvo and HERMES memory designs.

methodology.novelty_claim: OptiMUS introduces a novel connection graph and modular LLM agent structure to process problems with long descriptions and large data without long prompts, outperforming SOTA.

fronts.name: Adaptive LLM-Driven Evolutionary Search with RL and Structural Feedback
fronts.summary:
This research front is dedicated to advancing LLM-driven evolutionary search paradigms for automated algorithm design, moving beyond basic code generation to incorporate sophisticated control and feedback mechanisms. Key themes include Reinforcement Learning (RL)-infused evolution, adaptive search strategies, structural feedback from code analysis, co-evolution of algorithms and prompts, and novel program representations. These advancements aim to enhance the efficiency, robustness, and discovery capabilities of LLM-based systems across diverse domains such as combinatorial optimization, scientific computing (e.g., gravitational-wave detection), and meta-black-box optimization.

Significant contributions include EvoTune and Self-Developing, which leverage Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with Forward KL regularization to iteratively fine-tune LLM generators, yielding up to 15% better optimality gaps on Bin Packing and 4.3% accuracy gains on GSM8k. AdaEvolve introduces a three-tier adaptive controller, outperforming AlphaEvolve on Circle Packing by dynamically modulating exploration. LLaMEA-SAGE integrates Explainable AI (SHAP) on Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) features to provide structural feedback, guiding LLM mutations for faster convergence on MA-BBOB. Novel co-evolutionary frameworks demonstrate massive performance jumps, such as co-evolving Fireworks Algorithm operators and prompts achieving 100% on Aircraft Landing, and CAE reducing TSP optimality gaps by 2-5% through bi-dimensional structural-functional co-evolution. EvoLattice introduces a Quality-Diversity DAG for persistent internal-population evolution, outperforming FunSearch on NAS-Bench-Zero by preserving diversity and providing fine-grained feedback. Furthermore, TIDE decouples structural reasoning from parameter tuning, outperforming EoH and ReEvo on COPs, while BehaveSim uses Dynamic Time Warping on problem-solving trajectories to enforce behavioral diversity, reducing the TSP optimality gap by 7.8%.

This front is rapidly emerging, characterized by fundamental architectural innovations rather than incremental improvements. The trajectory indicates a strong push towards integrating diverse advanced mechanisms to create more robust, efficient, and capable LLM-driven algorithm discovery systems. Future work will likely focus on unifying these disparate advancements into holistic frameworks, scaling them to tackle larger and more complex real-world problems, and developing a stronger theoretical understanding of their emergent properties and convergence guarantees.
future_directions: Integrate RL-infused evolution techniques (e.g., EvoTune's DPO with Forward KL) with adaptive control mechanisms (e.g., AdaEvolve's accumulated improvement signal) to dynamically balance exploration and exploitation in LLM-driven search.

Develop unified frameworks that combine structural feedback from XAI on AST features (LLaMEA-SAGE) with behavioral diversity metrics (BehaveSim's DTW on problem-solving trajectories) to provide multi-faceted guidance for LLM mutations.

Extend co-evolutionary approaches (e.g., co-evolution of algorithms and prompts, structural-functional co-evolution) to multi-objective optimization problems, incorporating explicit diversity-promoting mechanisms like island-based evolution (C-Evolve).

Explore the application of persistent Quality-Diversity graph representations (EvoLattice) in conjunction with agentic meta-synthesis (SS-Logic's Generator-Validator pairs) for scalable co-evolution of problem instances and algorithms.

Investigate replacing Transformer-based population encoders in LLM-driven meta-heuristic search with more efficient architectures like Mamba blocks (L2E) to enable larger population sizes and improve scalability for complex problems.

fronts.name: AlphaEvolve and Execution-Guided Synthesis for Generalizable Algorithm and Operator Discovery
fronts.summary:
This research front focuses on advancing automated algorithm and operator design using LLMs, primarily within evolutionary frameworks like AlphaEvolve, FunSearch, and EOH. The core theme is the discovery of generalizable components, such as activation functions and retrieval algorithms, and the evolution of complex heuristics for domains like multi-objective and dynamic job shop scheduling. A significant emphasis is placed on refining the LLM's role beyond simple code generation, towards more sophisticated, interactive, and state-aware search processes.

Key contributions include Ouellette's (2025) GridCoder, which achieved 80% success on ARC-AGI out-of-distribution tasks via execution-guided neural program synthesis, significantly outperforming AlphaEvolve. E2OC (2026) introduced a hierarchical Monte Carlo Tree Search for co-evolving interdependent operators, demonstrating over 20% Hypervolume improvement on FJSP/TSP benchmarks. Vitvitskyi et al. (2026) successfully applied AlphaEvolve to discover novel activation functions, such as `GELU * (1 + 0.5 sinc(x))`, which improved CLRS-30 performance by +2.9%. Other advancements include semantics-aware crossover for symbolic regression, online LLM intervention for flexible job shop scheduling, and dual-agent 'Hybrid Evaluation' for dynamic assembly flow shop rules. Crucially, Berthold et al. (2026) provided a reality check, showing that standard global NLP solvers can surpass AlphaEvolve on its own combinatorial geometry benchmarks, underscoring the need for robust comparative evaluation.

This front is rapidly emerging, characterized by a diverse range of applications and a strong methodological focus on enhancing the LLM-evolutionary loop. The trajectory indicates a shift towards more intelligent LLM integration, incorporating state-conditioned decoders, semantic feedback, and hierarchical design strategies. Future research will likely concentrate on developing more rigorous benchmarking against classical optimization methods, improving the sample efficiency of LLM-driven search, and managing the complexity of evolved programs. The consistent emphasis on out-of-distribution generalization and the discovery of fundamental algorithmic components points to a foundational transformation in automated algorithm design.
future_directions: Extend execution-guided neural program synthesis (EG-NPS) beyond ARC-AGI to complex combinatorial optimization problems, integrating state-conditioned decoders for improved sample efficiency in AlgoEvo.

Implement hierarchical 'strategy-first' search layers, as seen in E2OC, to evolve semantic blueprints for interdependent operators before code generation, preventing local optima in multi-component heuristic design.

Develop and validate 'Small-Scale Lab' proxy evolution methodologies, optimizing on cheap, synthetic datasets to find generalizable logic for complex OR heuristics, as demonstrated by AlphaEvolve for activation functions.
Integrate 'On-Policy Negative Feedback' mechanisms, similar to CASTER, to actively train proxy reward models in AlgoEvo, enabling reliable offloading of expensive evaluations to cheaper proxies.

Rigorously benchmark LLM-discovered algorithms and operators against state-of-the-art classical global optimization solvers (e.g., SCIP, Xpress) to ensure true novelty and performance gains, as highlighted by Berthold et al.
top_affiliations: Southern University of Science and Technology

fronts.name: AlphaEvolve-Based Code Evolution for Algorithmic and Scientific Discovery
fronts.summary:
This research front is centered on LLM-driven code evolution, primarily building upon and extending the AlphaEvolve framework, alongside related approaches like FunSearch and Darwin Gödel Machine (DGM). The core theme is the autonomous discovery and optimization of complex algorithms and scientific solutions by evolving executable code. This involves LLMs generating, critiquing, and refining programs, often in a self-improving loop, to tackle challenging problems across domains such as combinatorial optimization, mathematical discovery, computer systems, and scientific simulation.

Key contributions include AlphaEvolve's pioneering work in diff-based full-file evolution and meta-prompt evolution, achieving state-of-the-art results in matrix multiplication and Google's production scheduling. DGM demonstrates open-ended evolution of self-improving agents by enabling architectural code changes via self-diagnosis, boosting SWE-bench performance from 20% to 50%. DeepEvolve augments AlphaEvolve with "Deep Research" (RAG-based mutation proposals) and iterative debugging, significantly improving execution success rates. Innovations in sample efficiency include ShinkaEvolve's novelty rejection sampling and DeltaEvolve's momentum-driven semantic deltas, reducing token consumption by ~37%. Architectural advancements like Magellan's hierarchical search (LLM for templates, external optimizer for parameters), ImprovEvolve's modular local search operators, and MadEvolve's differentiable inner-loop optimization further refine the LLM's role. EvoX introduces meta-evolution, jointly evolving solutions and search strategies, achieving state-of-the-art across nearly 200 tasks. Other notable results include FunSearch's 3058x reduction in Feynman integral seeding, SATLUTION's SAT solver evolution outperforming 2025 competition winners, and ArchAgent's 1-5% IPC gains in cache replacement policies.

This front is rapidly maturing, characterized by a proliferation of sophisticated architectural extensions and performance optimizations built upon established frameworks like AlphaEvolve. The consistent "MUST-READ | CHANGES-THINKING" tags across numerous papers indicate a high rate of impactful innovations. The trajectory points towards increasingly autonomous and sample-efficient systems capable of tackling larger, more complex, and multi-faceted problems. Future work will likely focus on integrating more advanced meta-learning (e.g., evolving the evolver's strategies, as in EvoX), enhancing robustness against "simulator escapes" and reward hacking, and developing mechanisms for true open-ended discovery that can generalize across diverse problem classes without extensive human intervention. The next papers will likely showcase systems that dynamically adapt their search strategies, leverage external knowledge more effectively, and seamlessly integrate with human experts for complex scientific and engineering challenges.
future_directions: Integrate meta-evolutionary loops (e.g., EvoX's two-level framework) to dynamically adapt search strategies and operators based on population state and stagnation detection, moving beyond fixed meta-heuristics.

Develop robust self-rectification and debugging agents (e.g., MAS^2's Rectifier, DeepEvolve's debugger) to automatically patch runtime errors and prevent regression in long-horizon code evolution, potentially using self-evolving rule systems like SATLUTION's.

Implement hierarchical search architectures (e.g., Magellan's template generation with external parameter tuning) and differentiable inner-loop optimization (e.g., MadEvolve's JAX integration) to separate structural code evolution from continuous parameter optimization, improving sample efficiency.

Enhance knowledge-guided evolution by integrating domain-specific RAG (e.g., GeoEvolve's GeoKnowRAG, DeepEvolve's Deep Research) to inject theoretical constraints and generate grounded mutation proposals, preventing search stagnation and ensuring theoretical soundness.

Explore test-time reinforcement learning with entropic objectives (e.g., ThetaEvolve, TTT-Discover) to fine-tune LLMs during inference for specific hard discovery tasks, optimizing for the single best solution rather than expected return.

fronts.name: Advanced LLM-Guided Co-Evolutionary Search for Combinatorial Optimization Heuristics
fronts.summary:
This front unifies research on enhancing LLM-based evolutionary search frameworks like FunSearch, EoH, and ReEvo for automated algorithm design. Key themes include multi-agent collaboration (RoCo, PathWise), co-evolution of components (G-LNS for destroy/repair, EvoPH for prompts/heuristics, ASRO for solver/instance), and novel search mechanisms (QUBE's offspring-aware selection, CALM's RL-finetuning, STRCMP's GNN-LLM fusion). Applications span combinatorial optimization problems such as TSP, CVRP, bin packing, and specialized domains like Bayesian Optimization acquisition functions and deletion-correcting codes.

Significant contributions include QUBE's UCB-based parent selection, outperforming FunSearch on OBP and TSP by 10-40% and 20% respectively, by focusing on offspring quality. CALM demonstrates RL-finetuning of LLMs (Qwen-7B) to generate superior heuristics for OBP and CVRP, beating GPT-4o baselines. PathWise introduces an Entailment Graph for planning, achieving robust performance on TSP, CVRP, and Bin Packing with half the evaluation budget of ReEvo and FunSearch. ASRO applies game theory to co-evolve solvers and adversarial instance generators, consistently beating static EoH baselines on TSPLIB and CVRPLIB. STRCMP integrates GNN structural priors with LLMs for MILP/SAT, reducing convergence time. Robusta improves FunSearch's worst-case performance on traffic engineering by 28x using an Analyzer-Explainer-Coder loop.

This front is rapidly maturing, moving beyond initial demonstrations of LLM code generation towards sophisticated, sample-efficient evolutionary architectures. The trajectory suggests a shift from simple prompt engineering to complex multi-agent systems, RL-finetuned LLMs, and game-theoretic co-evolution. The next wave of papers will likely focus on integrating these advanced mechanisms into unified, robust frameworks, improving scalability to higher-dimensional and multi-objective problems, and developing more rigorous theoretical foundations for LLM-driven search. Expect to see more work on automated curriculum generation and dynamic adaptation of search strategies based on problem characteristics.
future_directions: Extend ASRO's game-theoretic co-evolution to multi-objective optimization and incorporate teacher-student interactions for curriculum design.
Integrate STRCMP's GNN-based structural priors with PathWise's Entailment Graph for enhanced memory and planning in topology-heavy combinatorial problems.
Develop unified frameworks that combine CALM's RL-finetuning with QUBE's offspring-aware parent selection and HSEvo's hybrid tuning for improved sample efficiency and exploration.
Apply the D-ID3 architecture for local, state-dependent code generation to evolve adaptive repair operators for specific bottlenecks in ALNS-based VRP solvers.
Investigate the automated generation of dynamic training curricula for AlgoEvo using EoH-driven test function design (Achtelik et al.) and adversarial instance generation (Karimi et al.).

fronts.name: Agentic LLM Synthesis and Verification for OR Formulations and Algorithms
fronts.summary:
This research front focuses on leveraging large language models (LLMs), often within agentic frameworks or through fine-tuning, to generate and verify code for diverse Operations Research problems. Key frameworks include CP-Agent and MIRROR for constraint and optimization modeling, A-LAMP for MDP formulation, and LLaMoCo and DPO-based fine-tuning for automated algorithm design. The work spans problem domains such as combinatorial routing, job shop scheduling, mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) cut generation, and even multi-agent reinforcement learning for energy trading.

Significant contributions include CP-Agent achieving 100% accuracy on a clarified CP-Bench for constraint modeling, and MIRROR establishing a new state-of-the-art with ~72% pass@1 on NL4Opt and IndustryOR benchmarks using iterative adaptive revision and hierarchical RAG. EquivaMap introduces a novel LLM-based method for 100% accurate equivalence checking of MILP formulations by discovering linear variable mappings. LLaMoCo demonstrates that a fine-tuned 350M parameter model can drastically outperform GPT-4 Turbo in optimization code generation (e.g., +368% on Ieval for OPRO), while EvoCut generates acceleration cuts reducing MILP gaps by 17-57%. New benchmarks like DCP-Bench-Open, CO-Bench, and HeuriGym rigorously evaluate LLM capabilities, highlighting challenges such as overfitting to example instances and difficulties with complex feasibility constraints.

This front is rapidly emerging and maturing, with a strong emphasis on developing robust, verifiable, and efficient LLM-driven tools for OR. The trajectory indicates a shift from basic code generation to more sophisticated tasks like formal equivalence checking and automated cut generation. Future work will likely focus on improving the robustness of LLM-generated code against overfitting, enhancing their ability to handle complex feasibility constraints, and scaling these agentic and fine-tuned approaches to larger, more complex industrial problems, particularly addressing the context fragmentation challenges observed in complex heuristic design.
future_directions: Integrate EquivaMap's propose-mapping-and-verify loop for rigorous equivalence checking and DCP-Bench-Open's Multi-Instance Accuracy metric for robust evaluation in OR-Bench pipelines.

Implement DPO with Diversity-Aware Rank-based sampling or LLaMoCo's instruction tuning to fine-tune smaller LLMs, creating specialized 'mutator' models for evolutionary search and acceleration cut generation (e.g., EvoCut's LP separation check).

Enhance agentic frameworks by adopting MIRROR's structured revision tip mechanism and A-LAMP's granular decomposition schema (Parameters -> Objectives -> Variables -> Constraints) for improved automated problem instantiation and iterative refinement.

Develop new agentic frameworks that overcome context fragmentation and effectively integrate feedback for generating complex, multi-objective heuristics, addressing the limitations identified by the HeuriGym benchmark.
Explore the 'Differential Attention' mechanism for multi-agent critics to handle irrelevant interactions and improve scalability in large-scale, multi-agent optimization problems.
top_affiliations: Shanghai University of Finance and Economics

fronts.name: LLM-Driven Optimization Model Synthesis with Agentic Refinement and Structural Validation
fronts.summary:
This research front focuses on leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) for the automated formulation and code generation of optimization problems from natural language descriptions. A central theme is the development of sophisticated multi-agent systems (e.g., OptiMUS, OptimAI, GALA, LinearizeLLM) and structured intermediate representations (e.g., LLMOPT's five-element formulation, Lyu et al.'s Canonical Intermediate Representation, SyntAGM's grammar-aware system) to enhance the accuracy, robustness, and scalability of LLM-generated models. Crucially, the front also emphasizes the creation of rigorous benchmarks and advanced evaluation methodologies (e.g., OptiBench, MIPLIB-NL, ORGEval, DUALSCHOOL) to systematically assess and drive progress in this complex domain.

Key contributions include LLMOPT's multi-instruction fine-tuning with KTO for structured problem definition, and multi-agent systems like OptiMUS and OptimAI, which employ connection graphs and UCB-based debug scheduling for iterative refinement. Advanced search and refinement techniques involve Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with symbolic pruning (Astorga et al.) or prompt backpropagation (SolverLLM), alongside error-driven fine-tuning (MIND) and experience libraries (AlphaOPT). For code generation and reformulation, CHORUS utilizes metadata-augmented RAG, while SyntAGM and LinearizeLLM integrate compiler-in-the-loop validation and structural policies for exact linearizations. A significant impact comes from new evaluation paradigms like ORGEval's graph-theoretic isomorphism detection and DUALSCHOOL's Canonical Graph Edit Distance, which provide more robust metrics than simple execution, revealing critical flaws in widely used benchmarks (e.g., NL4Opt, IndustryOR, OptMATH, MIPLIB-NL).

This front is rapidly emerging, marked by a clear shift from basic prompt engineering to sophisticated multi-agent architectures, structured intermediate representations, and iterative self-correction loops. The increasing emphasis on rigorous benchmarking and structural/semantic validation signifies a strong maturation, as researchers move beyond superficial performance metrics to address fundamental challenges in model correctness and reliability. The next wave of research will likely integrate these advanced formulation and verification techniques into end-to-end evolutionary algorithm design systems, capable of not just generating models but also adapting them to dynamic, real-world stochastic environments, potentially leveraging insights from trajectory-aware meta-optimization (SOCRATES) and hierarchical heuristic evolution (MiCo).
future_directions: Integrate graph-theoretic structural validation (ORGEval, DUALSCHOOL) into evolutionary search fitness functions and benchmarking pipelines: Utilize Weisfeiler-Lehman tests and Canonical Graph Edit Distance for rapid, structural pruning of invalid candidates and robust evaluation of model equivalence, reducing reliance on expensive solver calls.

Develop 'Reverse Socratic' data synthesis pipelines (ReSocratic, OptMATH, ProOPF) for generating large-scale, verified OR benchmarks and training environments: Construct ground-truth formal models first, then back-translate to natural language with solver-based validation, ensuring solvability and accuracy for OR-Bench and AlgoEvo.

Implement advanced memory and learning mechanisms (Prompt Backpropagation, Condition Refinement, DFPO) in multi-agent LLM systems: Leverage SolverLLM's prompt backpropagation, AlphaOPT's applicability condition refinement, and MIND's error-driven policy optimization to improve sample efficiency and prevent recurrence of failed patterns in iterative model synthesis.

Design multi-agent frameworks that explicitly map natural language to high-level DSLs (RSOME) and solver-specific structures (OptiMUS-0.3): Train LLMs to utilize robust/stochastic programming libraries and detect specialized solver constraints (e.g., SOS, indicators) to enhance the robustness and efficiency of generated optimization models.

Extend LLM-driven meta-optimization (SOCRATES) and hierarchical heuristic evolution (MiCo) for adaptive algorithm design in stochastic environments: Evolve portfolios of specialized heuristics and dynamic selection policies, or use trajectory-aware reasoning to schedule hybrid algorithms, to handle non-stationary and diverse real-world problems.

fronts.name: Process-Supervised LLM Agents for Robust OR Model Synthesis and Verification
fronts.summary:
This research front focuses on developing advanced LLM-driven frameworks for automated optimization modeling and algorithm discovery, moving beyond basic code generation to robust, verifiable, and self-improving systems. Key frameworks like DPLM, OR-Agent, StepORLM, SIRL, CALM, NEMO, and ReLoop are central, emphasizing process-level feedback, semantic verification, and verifiable synthetic data generation to enhance the reliability and performance of LLM-generated OR solutions.

Key contributions include DPLM's DualReflect synthetic data pipeline for dynamic programming formulation, achieving +19.6% macro-average on DP-Bench with GPT-4o. OR-Agent introduced a tree-structured research workflow for algorithm discovery, showing a ~2x improvement over FunSearch and ReEvo on TSP benchmarks. StepORLM's Generative Process Reward Model (GenPRM) and Weighted DPO enabled an 8B model to beat GPT-4o by +29.6% Pass@1 accuracy on NL4Opt. SIRL's Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) and 'Partial KL' surrogate function achieved SOTA on OptMATH and IndustryOR. NEMO demonstrated execution-aware modeling with an asymmetric simulator-optimizer validation loop, reaching SOTA on 8/9 optimization benchmarks, while ReLoop's behavioral perturbation testing detected 'silent failures' and improved execution accuracy by +27.9pp on RetailOpt-190. Other notable works include SAC-Opt's backward-guided semantic alignment for ~22% accuracy improvement on ComplexLP, LLOME's Margin-Aligned Expectation (MargE) loss for constrained sequence optimization, and CALM's 'Intervener' model enabling a 4B model to match DeepSeek-R1 on OR benchmarks.

This front is rapidly emerging and maturing, with a clear trajectory towards more autonomous and trustworthy LLM agents for Operations Research. The focus is shifting from simple outcome-based rewards to sophisticated process supervision, multi-agent verification, and self-correction mechanisms. Future work will likely integrate these advanced verification and data generation techniques into unified, scalable agentic frameworks, pushing towards fully autonomous algorithm discovery and optimization modeling with minimal human oversight and enhanced reliability.
future_directions: Integrate Generative Process Reward Models (GenPRM) and Margin-Aligned Expectation (MargE) loss into evolutionary search frameworks for more stable and effective heuristic evolution.
Develop and scale 'Backward Generation' (Code→Problem) and 'Symbolic→NL' synthetic data pipelines to create massive, verifiable datasets for fine-tuning OR-specific LLMs.

Implement multi-agent verification loops using mutation testing, asymmetric simulator-optimizer validation, and behavioral perturbation testing to generate robust, ground-truth-free fitness signals for automated algorithm discovery.
Explore 'Partial KL' strategies and 'Intervener' models to balance LLM exploration with adherence to strict coding standards and enforce tool-use for generating executable and correct OR models.
Extend tree-structured research workflows and 'instrumentation-via-code' patterns to enable deeper diagnosis of failure modes and iterative refinement in complex OR problem-solving.
top_affiliations: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

fronts.name: Operations Research for LLM Serving and Agentic Code Evolution
fronts.summary:
This research front explores the critical intersection of Operations Research (OR) and AI, manifesting in two primary directions: applying advanced OR techniques to optimize Large Language Model (LLM) serving and infrastructure, and leveraging LLMs as intelligent agents for evolutionary search and high-performance code generation in complex problem domains. Key frameworks like Helix, Cascadia, SageServe, and MuxTune are optimizing LLM deployment and fine-tuning, while ParEVO, StitchCUDA, ARLArena, PAMA, and ETS are pioneering LLM-guided evolution and optimization.

Significant contributions include Helix and Cascadia's MILP-based solutions for dynamic LLM model placement and request routing on heterogeneous GPUs, achieving up to 3.3x throughput gains. SageServe demonstrates 25% GPU-hour savings in cloud LLM serving through ILP and ARIMA forecasting, while MuxTune boosts multi-task PEFT throughput by 5x via hierarchical multiplexing. In agentic evolution, ParEVO synthesizes parallel algorithms with 106x speedups using fine-tuned LLMs and MAP-Elites. StitchCUDA employs rubric-based agentic RL for GPU program generation, yielding 4.41x speedups, and ARLArena's SAMPO stabilizes multi-turn agentic RL, improving average scores by 25.2%. PAMA provides a convex optimization approach for multi-objective LLM alignment, and ETS uses ILP to reduce KV cache by 1.8x in LLM inference tree search.

This front is rapidly emerging, characterized by a deepening integration of mathematically rigorous OR methods with advanced AI capabilities. The trajectory indicates a strong move towards developing more robust theoretical guarantees for LLM-empowered OR, scaling these solutions to highly dynamic and distributed real-world systems, and enhancing the autonomy and efficiency of LLM-driven agents for code and heuristic discovery. Future work will likely focus on creating unified frameworks that seamlessly blend optimal resource allocation with intelligent, adaptive code generation.
future_directions: Extend Helix and Cascadia's MILP formulations to dynamically adapt to changing cluster conditions and integrate advanced LLM inference optimizations like speculative decoding.
Develop robust theoretical guarantees for LLM-empowered contract design (e.g., from Zhan et al.) and multi-objective alignment (e.g., PAMA) in non-convex or stochastic environments.
Integrate ParEVO's MAP-Elites and DPO-based fine-tuning with StitchCUDA's rubric-based RL and atomic skill decomposition for more sample-efficient and robust evolutionary code generation agents.
Apply GauS's Gaussian reparameterization technique to replace categorical relaxations in continuous latent-space optimization problems within LLM-guided search for ordered parameters.
Investigate a unified framework combining SageServe's ILP and ARIMA forecasting with SkyNomad's multi-region spot instance scheduling for cost-optimal and robust global LLM serving.

fronts.name: OR-Driven LLM System Optimization and Automated Algorithm Synthesis
fronts.summary:
This research front is defined by the synergistic application of Operations Research (OR) principles and advanced LLM capabilities to enhance the performance, efficiency, and robustness of AI systems. It encompasses two primary thrusts: leveraging formal OR methods (Integer Linear Programming, Linear Programming, SMT, primal-dual frameworks) for optimizing LLM serving, inference, and resource allocation, and employing LLMs within evolutionary or multi-agent frameworks for automated algorithm design, debugging, and system optimization. Key areas include dynamic rebatching, fine-grained MoE scheduling, hybrid parallelism, KV-cache management, model compression, robust resource provisioning, and iterative, feedback-driven LLM-guided design.

Key contributions in OR for LLM systems include HAP's ILP for dynamic hybrid parallelism (1.6x speedup), FinDEP and FineEP's LP for fine-grained MoE scheduling (up to 47.6% throughput gains), and MoETuner's ILP for topology-aware expert placement (17.5% speedup). PROBE introduces lookahead pipelining for 1.3x MoE speedup, while DREX achieves 2-12% throughput improvement via dynamic rebatching for early-exit LLMs. DualPath boosts KV-cache throughput by 1.87x. MC# utilizes ILP for mixed-precision MoE compression (6.2x weight reduction), and 3D-Learning employs diffusion models for distributionally robust LLM resource provisioning, reducing regret by 40-50%. Twill applies ILP/SMT to optimize GPU kernel scheduling, matching hand-tuned Flash Attention performance. In LLM-guided algorithm design, EvoStage uses multi-agent LLMs for stagewise algorithm design, outperforming AlphaEvolve. DORA improves test-time search efficiency by 3.5x FLOPs on MATH500 via semantic uniqueness reweighting. GRPO trains LLMs for iterative OR model debugging using solver diagnostics as dense rewards (95.3% recovery), and BAMF-AI4S uses Bayesian adversarial co-optimization for scientific code generation, outperforming GPT-4o. MaMa applies Stackelberg Security Games for adversarial co-evolution of safe agentic systems, reducing attack rates, and GEPA optimizes multi-agent system prompts by evolving the evaluator's prompt, improving pass rates by 7%.

This front is rapidly emerging and expanding, indicating significant foundational shifts from heuristic-based AI system management towards principled, OR-driven optimization, and from black-box LLM prompting to iterative, feedback-driven LLM-guided algorithm and system design. The trajectory suggests a strong focus on scaling OR solvers for real-time integration, developing more sophisticated LLM-OR coupling mechanisms, and incorporating multi-modal feedback into design loops. Future work will likely emphasize enhancing robustness and safety through adversarial co-evolution and distributionally robust optimization, as well as improving generalization across broader problem classes and non-stationary environments.
future_directions: Extend EvoStage to efficiently exploit intermediate information for one-time execution algorithms and integrate multi-modal feedback (e.g., 3D visualizations) for heuristic design.
Develop adaptive, real-time OR-based scheduling algorithms for dynamic LLM serving scenarios, incorporating transition overheads (HAP) and switching-aware fluid oracles (SP-UCB-OLP).
Integrate generative ambiguity sets (3D-Learning) and adversarial co-evolution loops (MaMa) into LLM-guided algorithm design frameworks to enhance robustness and safety.
Apply semantic uniqueness reweighting (DORA) and Bayesian surrogate modeling on code embeddings (BAMF-AI4S) to drastically improve sample efficiency in LLM evolutionary search.
Explore advanced planning solvers beyond greedy heuristics within lookahead pipelining architectures (PROBE) for dynamic MoE inference optimization.

fronts.name: Combinatorial Optimization and Hybrid RL for LLM Serving and GPU Scheduling
fronts.summary: This research front focuses on applying Operations Research (OR) and hybrid OR-Reinforcement Learning (RL) techniques to optimize resource allocation and scheduling for Large Language Model (LLM) inference and Deep Learning (DL) workloads. A central theme is the efficient management of heterogeneous and distributed computing environments, including disaggregated prefill-decode architectures, dynamic GPU multi-tenancy, and KV cache eviction. Key frameworks and approaches include J3O for hierarchical multi-task inference, MIGRator for dynamic Multi-Instance GPU reconfiguration, RLTune and HetRL for hybrid RL/MILP scheduling, TORTA for RL-supervised Optimal Transport, GoodSpeed for fair goodput optimization, Entropic-Time Inference for entropy-aware scheduling, and AMPD for multi-round LLM inference. Significant contributions include asymptotically optimal 'Gate-and-Route' policies for prefill-decode contention (Lin et al., ~30% lower revenue loss) and dynamic programming for batching in speculative decoding (Zhu et al., 30-45% latency reduction). For GPU scheduling, MIGRator achieved ~20% Goodput gains via ILP for NVIDIA MIG, while RLTune (PPO + MILP) reduced makespan by ~25% for DL workloads. HetRL demonstrated 3-9x throughput gains for RL training on heterogeneous GPUs using multi-level search. KV cache optimization saw 80% cache reduction with minimal degradation using combinatorial optimization (Tang et al.). QR-Adaptor used gradient-free search to outperform 4-bit QLoRA with 12% less memory. Furthermore, LLM-guided OR approaches like FLEET (LLM-extracted fitness matrices for MILP) outperformed pure LLM planners by ~7% on heterogeneous robot tasks, and MAESTRO used multi-agent LLMs with 'Exploration Reports' for materials discovery. This front is rapidly emerging, driven by the increasing complexity and scale of LLM deployments, particularly in distributed and heterogeneous settings. The trajectory is clearly towards more sophisticated hybrid OR-RL architectures that can handle dynamic, stochastic, and real-time resource management challenges. Future research will likely integrate these advanced optimization techniques with evolving LLM architectures, addressing critical aspects such as dynamic resource availability, fault tolerance, and energy efficiency. The emphasis will be on developing robust, online, and adaptive solutions that effectively bridge the gap between theoretical optimality and practical deployability in production AI systems.
future_directions: Extend Entropic-Time Inference to integrate with speculative decoding and non-autoregressive models for further throughput gains.
Develop RL-guided Solver patterns (like RLTune and TORTA) for GPUSched to handle multi-objective optimization and dynamic constraints in heterogeneous GPU clusters.
Apply Fidelity Sensitivity Profiling and Proxy Tuning from QR-Adaptor to accelerate evolutionary search for other LLM compression and architecture optimization tasks.
Generalize Gate-and-Route policies from Lin et al. (2602.02987) to heterogeneous GPU infrastructures and develop diffusion approximations for tail-latency SLI guarantees.
Integrate Exploration Report Agent from MAESTRO into AlgoEvo to enable self-learning search strategies from initial population generations.

fronts.name: Optimal Learning, Robust Decoding, and Agentic System Orchestration
fronts.summary:
This research front unifies advanced Operations Research (OR) and AI search techniques to optimize various facets of Large Language Models (LLMs) and multi-agent systems. Key themes include sequential optimal learning for prompt engineering (Wang et al.), convex minimization for LLM inference acceleration (Global Resolution), and robust multi-objective optimization for LLM alignment (RMOD). Furthermore, it explores sophisticated resource allocation strategies for LLM workloads (PromptTuner, tLoRA) and novel orchestration methods for multi-agent systems (APEMO, AgentDropoutV2), alongside neuro-symbolic search for complex design tasks (AI4S-SDS).

Specific contributions include Wang et al.'s sequential optimal learning framework, which leverages a Knowledge-Gradient policy to achieve 6.47-11.99% improvements in prompt engineering over evolutionary baselines. PromptTuner demonstrates 4-8x reductions in SLO violations for LLM prompt tuning through its 'Prompt Bank' and hierarchical scheduler. For LLM inference, the Global Resolution algorithm provides an exact, 10,000x faster solution for optimal multi-draft speculative sampling via convex minimization. In LLM alignment, RMOD formulates robust multi-objective decoding as a zero-sum game, yielding up to +1.5% worst-case reward. Multi-agent systems benefit from APEMO's dynamic compute reallocation, improving trajectory quality by up to 42.86%, and AgentDropoutV2's test-time pruning, boosting accuracy by ~6% on math benchmarks.

This front is clearly emerging, characterized by the introduction of novel OR formulations and AI search architectures to address fundamental challenges in LLM and agentic system performance, efficiency, and reliability. The papers demonstrate a shift from heuristic-driven approaches to more theoretically grounded, optimal, or robust solutions. The likely next papers will focus on integrating these disparate OR techniques into more comprehensive frameworks, scaling them to larger and more complex real-world LLM deployments, and exploring their application to new generative AI paradigms beyond current LLM architectures, with a strong emphasis on sample efficiency and robustness.
future_directions: Integrate Knowledge-Gradient (KG) policies or similar optimal learning strategies (from Wang et al.) into AlgoEvo for meta-parameter optimization or strategy selection to improve sample efficiency.

Adapt the 'Global Resolution' convex minimization approach (from 2511.15898) for selecting diverse solution subsets in evolutionary algorithms, leveraging polymatroid theory for discrete optimization.

Extend the RMOD framework's dynamic adversarial weighting (from 2503.08796) to balance conflicting objectives (e.g., runtime vs. solution quality) in multi-objective evolutionary search or multi-agent decision-making.

Develop a 'Failure-Driven Indicator Pool' (inspired by AgentDropoutV2, 2602.23258) to prune erroneous code generations or agent actions in AlgoEvo, using mined failure trajectories to build a repository of 'forbidden patterns'.

Apply the 'Sibling-Aware Expansion' and 'Sparse State Storage' mechanisms from AI4S-SDS (2603.03686) to scale search horizons and improve diversity in evolutionary code generation or complex planning tasks.
top_affiliations: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Centre for Perceptual and Interactive Intelligence

fronts.name: OR-Driven LLM Inference Scheduling and Agentic System Optimization
fronts.summary:
This research front is defined by the rigorous application of Operations Research (OR) principles, particularly Integer Linear Programming (ILP), Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP), and queueing theory, to two critical areas: optimizing LLM inference serving infrastructure and enhancing LLM-based agentic systems. For inference, papers like Bari et al. (2025), Li et al. (2025), Tian et al. (2025), and Pang et al. (2025) develop throughput-optimal and SLO-aware scheduling algorithms, often leveraging queueing models and bin-packing. For agentic systems, works such as BAMAS (2025), OSCAR (2026), and ConstraintLLM (2025) use ILP/MIP for resource allocation, planning, and code generation, while DHEvo (2025) and VisionCreator (2026) explore evolutionary and reinforcement learning paradigms guided by OR-inspired principles.

Key contributions include the development of throughput-optimal schedulers like RAD/SLAI (Bari et al.) and Staggered Batch Scheduling (Tian et al.) for LLM inference, demonstrating significant reductions in TTFT (e.g., 30-40%) and throughput increases (e.g., 20% over baselines). Memory-constrained scheduling is addressed by MC-SF (2026) and Nested WAIT (2026), achieving near-optimal latency and high-probability bounds against OOMs. For agentic systems, BAMAS (2025) achieved ~80% cost reduction in multi-agent LLM systems using ILP for model selection, while OSCAR (2026) used MIP to derive optimal planning trajectories for VLM-steered image retrieval.
